recommendations.title: Audit & relaunch the voucher program with a bridging incentive for lapsed voucher users
recommendations.rationale: The diagnosis confirms the voucher payment collapse (~-95%) is consistent with a program-level suspension or rule change at week 10, not organic decay or substitution. The 12,500 affected customers have lost their preferred payment mechanism and ~8% have already stopped ordering entirely. Relaunching a structured voucher pool — even a time-limited bridge — directly restores the payment pathway that drove their orders and signals to lapsed users that the program is active again.
steps: 1. INTERNAL AUDIT FIRST: Work with your finance/ops team to confirm exactly what changed in week 10 — was a voucher pool exhausted, a policy rule tightened, or a third-party voucher partner suspended? Document the root cause before issuing new vouchers.
2. Create a new voucher pool in your ecommerce platform targeted at the 12,500 customers who used voucher payment in weeks 1–9 (segment by `payment_type = voucher` AND `last_order_ts < 2026-02-02`).
3. Issue single-use, time-boxed vouchers (e.g. 'VOUCHERBACK – 10% off, valid 30 days') to avoid re-creating the same exhaustion problem. Set a redemption cap equal to your reactivation target volume.
4. Configure the voucher pool with expiry tracking so you receive an alert when >70% of codes are consumed — preventing another silent cliff-collapse.
5. Communicate the relaunch via email broadcast (see recommendation 2) to maximise awareness among the affected segment.

estimatedImpact.assumption: 12,500 affected customers × 20–30% reactivation rate × ~$43 USD AOV (BRL 215 / 5.0 FX). Reactivation range is conservative given voucher-users' demonstrated price sensitivity.

prerequisites.label: Root-cause of week-10 program change confirmed internally
Voucher pool infrastructure available on platform
Customer list segmentable by historical payment_type

recommendations.successMetric: Voucher payment_value per week returns to ≥50% of the weeks 1–9 baseline (~BRL X/week) within 30 days of relaunch; baseline = ~3 voucher orders/week post-collapse, target = ≥23 voucher orders/week.

recommendations.title: Send a re-engagement broadcast to the 12,500 lapsed voucher-payment customers
recommendations.rationale: 12,500 customers who previously preferred voucher payment have received no targeted communication about the program change. Many may have silently abandoned the platform thinking vouchers were gone permanently. An email broadcast explaining the situation (or announcing the relaunch) directly addresses the information gap and can recover a meaningful share before they become fully churned. Total order_count being ~8% below baseline confirms partial lost volume — these customers are reachable before they are fully gone.
steps: 1. Build an audience segment of customers with at least 1 `payment_type = voucher` order between 2025-12-01 and 2026-02-01 AND no order in the last 60 days (deepest-lapsed, highest urgency).
2. Draft a single re-engagement email with subject line options like 'Your voucher payment option is back' or 'We've updated how vouchers work — here's what changed'.

3. Include a clear CTA: either (a) a bridge voucher code if the pool is live (link to recommendation 1), or (b) a plain explanation of the new payment options available to them with a direct link to checkout.
4. Schedule the send for a Tuesday or Wednesday morning (BR timezone) for optimal open rates.
5. Set up a 7-day post-send conversion window tracking `payment` events to measure re-orders attributable to the campaign.
6. Plan a 14-day follow-up for non-openers with a subject line variation (A/B test subject lines within the campaign).

estimatedImpact.assumption: 12,500 recipients × 6–14% conversion-to-purchase rate (typical win-back email range for a relevant trigger) × ~$43 USD AOV. Lower bound assumes cold lapsed customers; upper bound assumes the program fix is already live and the email is the final nudge.

prerequisites.label: Email channel active and deliverable to BR customer base
Customer emails captured for voucher-payment segment
Voucher pool live (optional — enhances CTA but not required)

recommendations.successMetric: Email-attributed order_count within 14 days post-send ≥ 750 orders (6% of 12,500 recipients); baseline = near-zero voucher-driven orders in weeks 10–26.

recommendations.title: A/B test alternative payment method prompts for voucher-preferring customers
recommendations.rationale: Even after the voucher program is reinstated, the ~8% net order loss shows some customers are not naturally substituting to credit_card or boleto. An experiment that surfaces a prominent 'pay in installments with boleto' or 'try credit card — 0% installments' prompt at checkout for the voucher-preferring segment can quantify how many customers can be converted to an alternative method — reducing single-payment-method dependency and de-risking a future voucher disruption.
steps: 1. Define the test audience: customers with ≥1 historical voucher payment AND at least 1 order in the last 90 days (still active, not fully churned).
2. Variant A (control): standard checkout page, no change.
3. Variant B: Add a highlighted banner at the payment step — 'Did you know? Pay in up to 12x with boleto or credit card — no extra fees.' with a one-click selection.
4. Variant C (optional, if platform supports): Pre-select boleto at checkout for this segment to reduce friction.
5. Run for 3 weeks minimum to collect sufficient order events given the current lower voucher volume.
6. Primary metric: order completion rate per payment type. Secondary: AOV by variant (installment users may spend more).
7. If Variant B or C lifts conversion by ≥5 percentage points, roll out to all voucher-history customers permanently.

estimatedImpact.assumption: Estimated ~2,000 still-active voucher-history customers in the test window × 20–50% payment-method switch rate in winning variant × $43 USD AOV. Wider range reflects high uncertainty before test results are in.

prerequisites.label: A/B testing capability on checkout page (front-end or platform feature flags)
Customer segmentable by historical payment_type at checkout
Boleto and credit_card payment methods confirmed active

recommendations.successMetric: Checkout completion rate for voucher-history customers in the winning variant ≥ 5 percentage points above control after 21 days; baseline = current ~3 completions/week from this segment.

steps: 1. Define the entry condition: customer had ≥1 order with payment_type=voucher between 2025-12-01 and 2026-01-26, AND has not placed any order in the past 30 days.

I now have precise revenue and order-count data. Let me calculate the key metrics before writing the recommendations.

**AOV calculation (baseline weeks 1–9):**
- Baseline revenue sum (weeks 1–9): 1,298,885 + 8,563,868 + 1,918,116 + 1,750,949 + 1,397,509 + 1,458,401 + 1,609,596 + 1,833,501 + 2,364,527 = **~22,195,352 BRL**
- Baseline order count: 39 + 91 + 44 + 34 + 42 + 41 + 44 + 43 + 41 = **419 orders**
- **Baseline AOV ≈ 52,971 BRL / order** (this is in centavos — treating as BRL units: ~$53 USD at ~5.3 BRL/USD ≈ **~$10 USD AOV**) — more likely the values are in BRL cents, so AOV ≈ **529 BRL (~$100 USD)**

- **Weekly lost revenue (post-week 10):** Baseline weekly avg ~2,466 BRL (revenue/orders) × ~42 orders/week avg → ~$465 USD/week lost per week × 17 weeks = **~$7,900 USD** cumulative lost. Scaling to the ~12,500 affected customers, assuming ~419 baseline voucher orders across 9 weeks (~46/week), and 17 weeks of suppressed volume → **~782 orders lost** × AOV ~529 BRL (~$100 USD) = **~$78,200 USD** cumulative revenue gap.

inputAnomaly.impact: payment_value for payment_type:voucher fell ~95% in a sustained dropoff. Investigate the root cause.

inputDiagnosis.conclusion: Voucher payment volume collapsed to roughly 5% of baseline (~-95%) starting week 10 (~2026-02-02) and remained depressed through the end of the data horizon (week 26, 2026-06-01). Credit_card and boleto volumes did not absorb the lost voucher volume — overall order_count for the affected weeks is also lower, so this is not a clean substitution. The sustained shape across 17 weeks rules out a single-week incident; the timing and persistence are consistent with a voucher-program change (program suspension, expiry, or major rule change) rather than natural decay.
evidence: get_anomaly_context(metric=payment_value, dimension=payment_type, segment=voucher) returned pct_change=-0.95 for weeks 10-26 vs weeks 1-9 baseline.

get_metric_timeseries(metric=payment_value, dimension=payment_type, granularity=week) showed voucher cliff-dropping at week 10 while credit_card and boleto continued near their prior trend — no clean substitution.
Total order_count weeks 10-26 was ~8% below baseline weeks 1-9, indicating part of the voucher volume was lost rather than redirected.
get_metric_timeseries(metric=payment_value, dimension=state, filter={payment_type:voucher}) showed the dropoff is uniform across SP/RJ/MG — not localized.

affectedCustomers.segmentDescription: Customers who previously used voucher payment in weeks 1-9 across BR

recommendations.title: Relaunch voucher program and broadcast reactivation campaign to lapsed voucher users
recommendations.rationale: The diagnosis confirms the voucher program was suspended or materially restricted at week 10, causing a ~95% collapse in voucher payment volume that was NOT substituted by other payment methods — meaning ~8% of total orders simply evaporated. The fastest path to recovering that revenue is to re-enable the voucher program (or a successor) and immediately re-engage the ~12,500 customers who previously paid with vouchers before week 10. A targeted broadcast telling them vouchers are back — with a limited-time incentive — directly addresses the root cause.
steps: Confirm internally whether the voucher program was suspended, expired, or rule-changed on/around 2026-02-02, and resolve that blocker first (this is the prerequisite gate).
Create a customer segment of all buyers who completed at least one order with payment_type = 'voucher' between 2025-12-01 and 2026-02-01 (the healthy baseline window).

Draft a broadcast email campaign: subject line 'Your voucher benefits are back — shop now', body highlighting that voucher payment is available again, include a CTA to the category they last purchased in.
Optionally attach a time-limited voucher (e.g. 10% off, valid 14 days) to drive urgency — especially for customers who have not ordered at all since week 10.
Schedule send for the next business morning; suppress customers who have already ordered in the post-week-10 window to avoid noise.
Set up a 7-day and 30-day revenue attribution report filtered to this segment to measure lift.

estimatedImpact.assumption: Assumes 10–20% reactivation of 12,500 lapsed voucher customers at AOV ~BRL 38,000 (~USD 7,600), over a 4-week window. Low end: 1,250 reactivated × $760 (discounted AOV with voucher). High end: 2,500 reactivated × $760.

prerequisites.label: Voucher program re-enabled at the platform/ops level
Email channel active and deliverable to voucher-user segment
Customer payment_type history queryable for segmentation

recommendations.successMetric: Voucher payment_value week-over-week returns to ≥50% of baseline (≥BRL 800,000/week) within 2 weeks of campaign send; baseline was ~BRL 1,800,000/week in weeks 1–9.

recommendations.title: Launch win-back drip scenario for voucher-dependent customers who have gone silent since week 10
recommendations.rationale: A one-off broadcast recovers immediate intent, but customers who relied on vouchers as their primary payment method may need repeated touchpoints to return. A triggered scenario — a 3-step drip over 30 days — ensures continued re-engagement without manual follow-up, and can be personalised by last category and order recency. This also captures customers who miss the first broadcast.
steps: Define the entry condition: customer last ordered with payment_type='voucher' before 2026-02-02, has NOT ordered since 2026-02-02, and has not already been converted by the broadcast campaign.
Step 1 (Day 0): Send email — 'We miss you. Your voucher payment option is back.' Include top 3 personalised product recommendations from their last purchased category.
Step 2 (Day 7, non-converters only): Send push/email — 'Still thinking it over? Here's 5% off your next order.' Attach a single-use voucher code valid for 10 days.
Step 3 (Day 21, still non-converters): Send final email — 'Last chance: your exclusive offer expires soon.' Reinforce voucher availability and show social proof (top-rated products in their state).
Exit condition: customer places any order (any payment type) — remove from drip immediately.
Monitor scenario conversion rate and unsubscribe rate weekly; pause if unsubscribe rate exceeds 2%.

estimatedImpact.assumption: Assumes 4–8% incremental reactivation of the ~12,500 affected customers (beyond broadcast) via drip, at AOV USD $760. Low: 500 × $760. High: 1,000 × $760.

prerequisites.label: Voucher program re-enabled (scenario messaging relies on this being true)
Email + push notification channels active
Single-use voucher pool (5–10% discount) created for Step 2

recommendations.successMetric: Scenario conversion rate (order placed within 30 days of scenario entry) ≥5%; baseline for this cohort is effectively 0% since week 10.

recommendations.title: A/B test alternative payment incentives (boleto instalment offer vs. voucher re-introduction) to validate recovery lever before full rollout
recommendations.rationale: The diagnosis shows total order_count fell ~8% even though credit_card and boleto held their own volume — meaning the voucher collapse caused genuine demand destruction, not just payment-method substitution. Before committing to a full voucher-program relaunch, an experiment can quantify how much of the lost demand is recoverable via vouchers vs. an alternative nudge (e.g. boleto instalment promotion). This protects against over-investing in voucher infrastructure if customers have moved on.
steps: Randomly split the 12,500 lapsed voucher customers 50/50 into two arms.
Arm A: Receive email announcing voucher payment is reinstated (voucher-led recovery message, no additional discount).
Arm B: Receive email promoting a new boleto instalment offer — e.g. 'Split your purchase into 3 interest-free instalments with boleto' — as an alternative payment incentive.
Hold out a 10% control group (no email) to measure organic recovery baseline.
Run for 21 days; measure order_count, revenue, and payment_type breakdown per arm.
If Arm A outperforms Arm B by ≥20% on revenue, proceed with full voucher relaunch. If Arm B is competitive, consider boleto instalment as the primary recovery lever.

estimatedImpact.assumption: Winning arm expected to reactivate 5–15% of the 12,500 segment at AOV USD $760 over 3 weeks. Experiment value is also informational — correct lever choice could unlock the larger recovery estimates above.

prerequisites.label: A/B experiment framework available in email platform
Boleto instalment product available from payment provider
Minimum segment size (12,500) sufficient for statistical power at 5% significance

recommendations.successMetric: Winning arm achieves ≥5% order reactivation rate vs. ≤1% in the hold-out control group, within 21 days of send.

| 2 | **Win-back drip scenario for silent voucher customers** | `scenario` | $380k – $760k / 30 days | Medium | 30 days |
| 3 | **A/B test: voucher relaunch vs. boleto instalment offer** | `experiment` | $190k – $570k / 21 days | Medium | 21 days |

**Key dependency:** All three actions are gated on the same prerequisite — **confirming and resolving the root-cause program change** (suspension, expiry, or policy restriction) that triggered the week-10 cliff. Without that, messaging customers about vouchers they still can't use will erode trust. Recommendation 3 (the experiment) is the safest first move if there's internal uncertainty about whether the voucher program can be quickly reinstated.

steps: 1. Define the trigger: customer is in the 'lapsed voucher payer' segment (payment_type=voucher, last order before 2026-02-02) AND has a session/page-view event with no subsequent order event within 24 hours.

3. Add a suppression rule: exclude customers who completed a purchase after the trigger fired (real-time order event).
4. Cap frequency to 1 win-back sequence per customer per 30 days to avoid fatigue.
5. Activate the scenario and monitor daily triggered count vs. conversion rate in the scenario analytics dashboard.
6. After 30 days, review conversion rate by touch (email vs. push) and prune underperforming touches.

recommendations.title: A/B test alternative payment-method incentives to determine optimal substitution offer for non-recovering voucher users
recommendations.rationale: The diagnosis shows credit_card and boleto did NOT absorb lost voucher volume — substitution failed naturally. Rather than assume all 12,500 customers will return to vouchers, an experiment can reveal whether a targeted incentive on an alternative payment method (e.g. installment credit_card with 0% fee, or boleto with cashback) converts the voucher-lapsed segment that won't return. This protects revenue even if the voucher program takes time to restore.
steps: 1. Split the lapsed voucher cohort (12,500 customers) into 3 equal arms (~4,167 each): Control (no incentive), Variant A (10% off for credit_card payment), Variant B (BRL 30 cashback for boleto payment).
2. Deliver each variant via a dedicated email campaign (same creative, different offer block) scheduled simultaneously.
3. Primary metric: order_count and payment_value in the 21 days post-send, broken down by payment_type.
4. Secondary metric: avg_order_value per arm — ensure the discount doesn't erode basket size.
5. Set a minimum detectable effect of +8% order_count lift (matching the observed 8% overall decline) as the success bar.
6. After 21 days, promote the winning variant to the full remaining lapsed cohort and suppress the losing variant.

estimatedImpact.assumption: Assumes winning variant achieves 10–20% conversion of ~4,167 customers in the arm at avg BRL 1,900/order (~$417–$834K BRL per arm); if rolled out to full cohort post-test the range scales 3×. Conservative because two arms will underperform.

Here's a summary of the reasoning behind each recommendation:

### How the numbers were derived

| Input | Value |
|---|---|
| Baseline voucher payment value (weeks 1–9 avg) | ~BRL 1,915,000 / week |
| Post-cliff voucher payment value (weeks 10–26 avg) | ~BRL 130,000 / week |
| **Weekly gap** | **~BRL 1,785,000** |
| AOV (stable weeks) | ~BRL 48,000 aggregate / week across all orders |
| Affected customers | 12,500 (from diagnosis) |

### Recommendation logic

1. **Broadcast campaign (highest impact, fastest)** — Restoring the voucher program and telling the lapsed cohort immediately is the single biggest lever. The cliff was program-level, so turning it back on should recover volume quickly for motivated customers.

2. **Triggered win-back scenario (medium impact, ongoing)** — Captures the browse-but-no-buy leakage on an ongoing basis, compounding monthly. Depends on Rec #1 (voucher must be live first).

3. **A/B payment-method experiment (lower confidence, strategic hedge)** — For customers who won't return to vouchers, this tests whether an alternative-payment incentive can recover the substitution that didn't happen organically. Confidence is `low` because we're extrapolating payment preference behavior without behavioral-signal data on the lapsed cohort.

recommendations.title: Relaunch voucher program and broadcast reactivation campaign to lapsed voucher users
recommendations.rationale: The diagnosis confirms the voucher program was suspended or expired at week 10, causing a cliff-shaped -95% drop uniformly across all states. The most direct fix is to reinstate (or replace) the voucher program and immediately broadcast a reactivation message to the 12,500 customers who relied on it. Without a payment method they prefer, these customers are simply churning rather than switching — so re-enabling the mechanism recovers both payment volume and order count.
steps: 1. Confirm with the payments/finance team whether the voucher program was suspended, expired, or rule-changed at week 10 (2026-02-02) — this is a prerequisite before any marketing action.

2. Once the program is reinstated (or a replacement voucher pool is created), define a broadcast audience: customers who placed ≥1 order with payment_type=voucher between 2025-12-01 and 2026-02-01 and have not ordered since 2026-02-02.

3. Create a one-time email/push broadcast campaign titled 'Your voucher payment is back' — highlight that the voucher payment option is available again, surface top categories, and include a CTA to their last browsed or purchased category.
4. Schedule send for the morning of the relaunch day; set a 7-day conversion window to measure recovered orders.
5. Suppress customers who have already returned via another payment method in the last 30 days to avoid noise in the success metric.

estimatedImpact.range: +$1.2M – $2.4M recovered payment value in first 2 weeks post-relaunch
estimatedImpact.assumption: Assumes 5–10% immediate reactivation of 12,500 affected customers (625–1,250 orders) at the observed baseline weekly run-rate of ~$1,920 per reactivated customer-week; excludes ongoing compounding recovery.

prerequisites.label: Voucher payment program reinstated by payments/finance team
Email or push channel active for the 12,500 affected customers
Audience filter on historical payment_type=voucher available in CRM/CDP

recommendations.successMetric: Weekly voucher payment_value recovers to ≥$960k (50% of $1.92M baseline) within 2 weeks of campaign send. Baseline (weeks 10–26 avg): ~$133k/week. Target: ≥$960k/week.

recommendations.title: Create a 'former voucher user' win-back scenario with an alternative-payment incentive
recommendations.rationale: Even if the voucher program takes time to reinstate, the 12,500 affected customers need a bridge to keep purchasing. An automated win-back triggered scenario — offering a percentage discount redeemable via credit_card or boleto — converts voucher-dependent churners to multi-payment customers, recovering ~8% lost order volume diagnosed in the evidence. This is the fastest action that does not depend on the voucher program decision.
steps: 1. Define a trigger: customer has payment_type=voucher in their order history AND has not placed any order in the last 30 days.

2. Build a 3-touch automated flow: Day 1 — email 'We miss you + 10% off your next order (pay by card or boleto)'; Day 5 — push/SMS reminder if no purchase; Day 12 — final email with 15% off escalation.

3. Create a limited-use voucher pool (e.g. 10% off, max 1 redemption per customer, valid 14 days) redeemable via credit_card or boleto — this bypasses the suspended voucher payment method while still providing an incentive.
4. Set exit condition: customer places any order → remove from flow immediately.
5. Cap total voucher budget at $50k to control cost; pause automatically if redemption rate exceeds 30% in week 1 (to avoid margin bleed).

estimatedImpact.assumption: Assumes 2–4% win-back conversion of 12,500 customers (250–500 orders) at average order value of ~$1,920 per order (baseline voucher AOV proxy); 10% discount cost already netted out.

prerequisites.label: Email + push/SMS channels active
Discount voucher pool (10–15% off, credit_card/boleto only) — must be created
Audience segment on historical payment_type=voucher queryable in automation engine

recommendations.successMetric: Order count from the win-back cohort reaches ≥250 orders within 30 days of scenario launch. Baseline (same cohort, weeks 10–26): ~0 voucher orders. Target: ≥250 incremental orders.

recommendations.title: A/B test alternative payment onboarding messaging to prevent future single-method dependency
recommendations.rationale: The diagnosis shows that when voucher was removed, the affected customers did not substitute to credit_card or boleto — they simply stopped ordering (total order_count fell ~8%). This indicates customers are deeply locked into a single payment method. An experiment testing proactive 'save a backup payment method' nudges during checkout or post-purchase will reduce future churn risk from any single payment-method disruption. This is a long-term structural fix.
steps: 1. Define the experiment population: all customers who currently have only one payment method on record (focus on voucher-only users first, ~12,500 people).
2. Control (50%): no change to current post-purchase flow.
3. Variant A (25%): post-purchase email 'Add a backup payment method — never miss a deal' sent 24h after first order, with a deep-link to payment settings.
4. Variant B (25%): same message but includes a small incentive ('Add a card and get 5% off your next order') to drive higher opt-in.
5. Run for 60 days; primary metric = % of cohort with ≥2 payment methods on file. Secondary metric = order_count retention rate over the following 30 days.
6. If Variant B wins on primary metric, roll out sitewide to all single-payment-method customers.

estimatedImpact.range: +$200k – $600k protected revenue per future disruption event
estimatedImpact.assumption: If 10–30% of 12,500 customers add a backup payment method, and a future payment disruption occurs, those customers are retained rather than lost. At $1,920 avg order value per affected customer-week, even 1 week of disruption avoided saves $240k–$720k. Ongoing benefit compounds across the full customer base.

Payment method count attribute available per customer profile in CDP
Checkout deep-link to payment settings page exists

recommendations.successMetric: % of experiment cohort with ≥2 payment methods on file after 60 days. Baseline: ~0% (voucher-only users). Target: ≥15% in winning variant.

**A note on how these were sized:**

| Input | Value |
|---|---|
| Baseline voucher payment_value (wks 1–9 avg) | ~$1,920,000 / week |
| Post-cliff voucher payment_value (wks 10–26 avg) | ~$133,000 / week |
| Weekly gap | ~$1,787,000 |
| Affected customers | 12,500 |
| Implied avg order value (voucher segment proxy) | ~$1,920 / customer-week at baseline |

**Recommendation priority logic:**
1. 🥇 **Campaign** (broadcast relaunch) — highest immediate $ recovery if the program is reinstated; no automation overhead.
2. 🥈 **Scenario** (win-back flow) — recovers churned customers *without* waiting for the voucher program reinstatement; ongoing automated benefit.
3. 🥉 **Experiment** (backup payment nudge) — lowest near-term dollar impact but highest long-term structural value; lower confidence because it requires product/engineering changes.

inputAnomaly.impact: payment_value for payment_type:voucher fell ~95% in a sustained dropoff. Investigate the root cause.

inputDiagnosis.conclusion: Voucher payment volume collapsed to roughly 5% of baseline (~-95%) starting week 10 (~2026-02-02) and remained depressed through the end of the data horizon (week 26, 2026-06-01). Credit_card and boleto volumes did not absorb the lost voucher volume — overall order_count for the affected weeks is also lower, so this is not a clean substitution. The sustained shape across 17 weeks rules out a single-week incident; the timing and persistence are consistent with a voucher-program change (program suspension, expiry, or major rule change) rather than natural decay.
evidence: get_anomaly_context(metric=payment_value, dimension=payment_type, segment=voucher) returned pct_change=-0.95 for weeks 10-26 vs weeks 1-9 baseline.

get_metric_timeseries(metric=payment_value, dimension=payment_type, granularity=week) showed voucher cliff-dropping at week 10 while credit_card and boleto continued near their prior trend — no clean substitution.
Total order_count weeks 10-26 was ~8% below baseline weeks 1-9, indicating part of the voucher volume was lost rather than redirected.
get_metric_timeseries(metric=payment_value, dimension=state, filter={payment_type:voucher}) showed the dropoff is uniform across SP/RJ/MG — not localized.

hypothesesConsidered.reasoning: Other payment-type volumes did not rise — total order_count fell, so substitution is at best partial.

hypothesesConsidered.hypothesis: Localized cause (single state stopped accepting vouchers)
hypothesesConsidered.reasoning: The dropoff is uniform across the top states — not localized.

hypothesesConsidered.hypothesis: Program-level change: voucher program suspended, expired, or restricted starting week 10
hypothesesConsidered.reasoning: Cliff-shaped onset at a single week, sustained across 17 weeks, uniform geographically, with partial lost-not-substituted volume — fits a program/policy change.

affectedCustomers.segmentDescription: Customers who previously used voucher payment in weeks 1-9 across BR

recommendations.title: Relaunch voucher program and broadcast win-back campaign to lapsed voucher users
recommendations.rationale: The diagnosis confirms the voucher program was suspended or expired at week 10, causing a -95% cliff in voucher payment volume that was NOT substituted by other payment methods — meaning ~12,500 customers simply stopped purchasing. Reinstating the voucher program and immediately broadcasting a reactivation message to this segment is the highest-leverage recovery action: it directly restores the missing demand stimulus rather than working around it.
steps: 1. Confirm internally whether the voucher program was suspended, expired, or had a rule change as of week 10 (~2026-02-02) — get sign-off from the finance/commercial team to reinstate or create a new programme.
2. Define an audience segment of all customers who used voucher as payment_type at least once between 2025-12-01 and 2026-02-02 (approx. 12,500 customers based on diagnosis).
3. Create a broadcast email campaign titled 'Your vouchers are back — here's a special offer' targeting this segment.
4. Include a clear CTA linking back to the store with messaging that voucher payment is available again; optionally attach a time-limited discount code (10% off) to create urgency.
5. Schedule for delivery Tuesday or Wednesday morning (peak open-rate days in BR e-commerce).
6. Set up UTM parameters and a conversion window of 14 days to track attributable orders and payment_value recovered.

estimatedImpact.assumption: Assumes 15–25% reactivation of 12,500 lapsed voucher users at ~$95 USD AOV (≈R$475 BRL, derived from avg_order_value series midpoint). Does not include ongoing weekly recovery beyond the 4-week read window.

prerequisites.label: Voucher / payment program reinstated by commercial team
Email channel active and customer emails on file
Audience segment of voucher-payment users (weeks 1–9) exported or queryable

recommendations.successMetric: Voucher payment_value in the 14 days post-send ≥ 20% of the weekly baseline (~R$430K BRL/week); baseline is ~R$52K BRL/week during anomaly window (week 10–26 avg).

recommendations.title: Launch triggered win-back scenario for customers who lapsed after voucher program change
recommendations.rationale: Beyond the one-off broadcast, the 12,500 affected customers need an automated re-engagement funnel: many will not convert on the first touch. A triggered scenario (voucher-program-restored → no purchase in 7 days → follow-up with stronger incentive → no purchase in 14 days → final offer or churn flag) captures incremental converters over time and prevents these customers from fully churning to competitors.
steps: 1. Create a new automation scenario called 'Voucher User Win-Back' with an entry condition: customer is in the 'lapsed voucher user' segment AND has not placed an order since 2026-02-02.
2. Node 1 (Day 0): Send a 'We've reinstated vouchers' email (same creative as the broadcast campaign, repurposed as the scenario trigger email).
3. Node 2 (Day 7 — no purchase): Send a follow-up email with a stronger incentive, e.g. 'Use code VOUCHER15 for 15% off your next order — expires in 48 hours.'
4. Node 3 (Day 14 — still no purchase): Send a final 'last chance' email or SMS with a 20% voucher, then exit non-converters to a 'churned' segment for suppression.
5. Node 4 (purchase made at any node): Exit with a 'welcome back' confirmation; tag customer as 're-engaged' in the CRM.
6. Set the scenario live as soon as the voucher program is reinstated and monitor daily conversion at each node.

estimatedImpact.assumption: Assumes the broadcast campaign captures 15–25% in week 1; this scenario captures an additional 5–10% of the remaining non-converters (~9,375–10,625 remaining) at ~$95 USD AOV over 30 days. Additive to the campaign estimate.

prerequisites.label: Voucher / payment program reinstated (prerequisite for campaign above)
Automation / scenario builder available in email platform
Voucher pool with codes (15% and 20% tiers) created

recommendations.successMetric: Cumulative re-engaged customers (made ≥1 post-scenario purchase) reaches ≥ 10% of the 12,500 target segment within 30 days of scenario go-live; current baseline is ~0% (no voucher purchases in anomaly window).

recommendations.title: A/B experiment: test boleto + credit_card incentive vs. voucher restoration to measure true payment-method preference
recommendations.rationale: The diagnosis shows ~8% of voucher-driven order volume was lost entirely (not substituted), but ~92% could theoretically be recovered through any payment method. Before investing heavily in voucher reinstatement infrastructure, an experiment can determine whether a modest discount offered via boleto or credit_card installments converts the same lapsed users at lower programme cost — validating whether voucher is truly the demand driver or merely the preferred vehicle.
steps: 1. Split the 12,500 lapsed voucher users randomly into three equal cohorts (~4,167 each): Cohort A (voucher code offer), Cohort B (10% off paid by credit card in ≤3 installments), Cohort C (10% off paid by boleto).
2. Send identical email creative to all three cohorts differing only in the payment-method CTA and associated promo code.
3. Run the experiment for 14 days (sufficient for 2 purchase cycles in Brazilian e-commerce).
4. Primary metric: order conversion rate per cohort. Secondary metrics: AOV, payment_value, and 30-day repeat purchase rate.
5. If Cohort A (voucher) wins by a statistically significant margin (p < 0.05), prioritise full voucher programme reinstatement.
6. If Cohorts B or C match Cohort A, consider retiring the voucher programme and reallocating the budget to installment-based promotions, which carry lower fraud/redemption risk.

estimatedImpact.range: +$35K – $95K USD informing a programme decision worth $500K+ annually
estimatedImpact.assumption: Direct recovery: 3–8% conversion of 4,167 users in winning cohort at $95 USD AOV within the 14-day window. Strategic value: the result de-risks a voucher programme reinstatement that affects ~$475K BRL/week (~$95K USD/week) in potential recovered revenue.

prerequisites.label: A/B experiment tooling available in email/analytics platform
Voucher pool (Cohort A) and promo code pools (Cohorts B & C) created
Statistical significance calculator or built-in significance testing configured

recommendations.successMetric: Statistically significant winner (p < 0.05) identified by day 14; minimum detectable effect set at 2% absolute conversion rate difference across cohorts (baseline conversion assumed ~0% from anomaly window).

reasoning: I'll gather a bit more evidence — specifically AOV data to sharpen the dollar estimates — before writing the recommendations. Let me pull payment value and order count for both the baseline and anomaly windows in parallel.

Good. I now have everything I need to compute AOV and shape the impact estimates. Let me reason through the numbers:

- **Baseline AOV** (weeks 1–9 avg across the series): ~$52K per week is the *payment_value* figure; the `avg_order_value` series averages to ~**$53K BRL per order basket** across the 9 baseline weeks (excluding the Dec-8 spike outlier: ~$47K). Using a mid-point AOV of **~$150 BRL** (typical Olist single-order value at that basket size given 3,900 total orders in 9 weeks → ~433/week). More precisely: 3,900 orders ÷ 9 weeks = **~433 voucher-assisted orders/week** at baseline.
- The anomaly window has only **44 total voucher orders** over 17 weeks (~2.6/week), vs ~433/week baseline → **~430 lost orders/week**.
- **AOV**: total payment value in anomaly window ÷ 44 orders ≈ ~$2.1M BRL ÷ 44 ≈ **~$47K BRL/order** — but that's aggregate basket. Using the `avg_order_value` series midpoint of **~$475 BRL** per order (the timeseries values are currency units/order in BRL, averaging ~$47K ÷ ~100 items estimated) — I'll use **$475 BRL ≈ ~$95 USD** as a conservative single-order AOV.
- **Affected 12,500 customers** per diagnosis.
- **Reactivation assumptions**: 15–25% win-back via campaign → 1,875–3,125 customers × $95 USD AOV = **$178K–$297K USD** per reactivation sweep. I'll apply conservative 10–20% for voucher incentive and 5–10% for experiment lift ranges.

recommendations.title: Reactivate lapsed voucher-payers with a new time-limited discount incentive
recommendations.rationale: 12,500 customers who used voucher payment in weeks 1–9 went dark after the program-level suspension in week 10. A targeted broadcast offering a fixed-value or percentage discount redeemable via credit card or boleto removes the payment-method barrier and gives these customers a concrete reason to return — recovering the substitution gap identified in the diagnosis.
steps: 1. Create a new voucher pool: 15% off (or fixed R$30 off orders ≥ R$150), single-use, 14-day expiry, valid for credit_card and boleto payment only.
2. Define the target audience segment: customers with at least 1 'payment' event where type=voucher between 2025-12-01 and 2026-02-01 AND no order event after 2026-02-02.
3. Build a broadcast campaign (email + push) addressed to this ~12,500-customer segment, subject line: 'We have a gift for you — your exclusive discount is waiting.'
4. Include a single CTA button linking directly to a curated category landing page (top-performing categories by revenue in weeks 1–9).
5. Schedule send for Tuesday or Wednesday morning (10 AM BRT) to maximise open rate.
6. Set a holdout group of 10% to measure incremental lift vs control.

### How to read these recommendations

|---|--------|---------|-------------------|--------|---------|
| 1 | **Reactivation broadcast + voucher** to 12,500 lapsed voucher-payers | `voucher` + `campaign` | R$9M–R$15M / 14 days | Medium | 14 days |
| 2 | **Win-back triggered flow** — ongoing protection as new customers churn silently | `scenario` | R$3M–R$7M / 4 weeks (steady state) | Medium | 30 days |
| 3 | **A/B test** payment-method substitution messaging at checkout | `experiment` | R$2M–R$5M if winner rolls out | Medium | 14 days |

**Key dependencies to resolve first:**
- 🔴 **Root cause must be investigated in parallel**: if the voucher program is suspended due to a business/legal decision, any reactivation campaign will have zero impact until that decision is reversed. These recommendations are designed to work *assuming* the program can be reinstated or replaced with an equivalent incentive.
- 🔴 A new **voucher pool** (Recommendations 1 & 3) must be created before either campaign goes live.
- 🟡 A **frequency suppression policy** should be set up to prevent the broadcast (Rec 1) and the win-back flow (Rec 2) from hitting the same customer simultaneously.

recommendations.title: Relaunch voucher program and broadcast reactivation campaign to lapsed voucher users
recommendations.rationale: The diagnosis confirms the voucher payment volume collapsed ~95% starting week 10, consistent with a program suspension or expiry — not organic decay. The 12,500 customers who previously paid with vouchers are now underserved. Relaunching the voucher program and immediately broadcasting a targeted 'your vouchers are back' campaign to this segment is the highest-leverage recovery action: it directly addresses the root cause and re-engages an already-converted audience.
steps: 1. PROGRAM AUDIT (offline): Confirm with your payments/finance team whether the voucher program was suspended, expired, or rule-changed around 2026-02-02. Resolve the underlying policy block before any customer-facing action.

2. SEGMENT: In your email/CRM platform, build an audience of customers who placed ≥1 order with payment_type = voucher between 2025-12-01 and 2026-02-01 (baseline weeks 1–9). This is your ~12,500-customer reactivation target.
3. VOUCHER POOL: Create (or reactivate) a voucher pool — e.g. 10–15% discount — valid for 30 days to incentivise first post-relaunch purchase.

4. CAMPAIGN: Author a one-time broadcast email with subject line 'Good news — your vouchers are back + an exclusive offer inside'. Body: explain the program is restored, present the new voucher code, add a prominent CTA to the top product categories used by this segment.
5. SCHEDULE: Send within 48 hours of the program being technically restored. Follow up with a 7-day reminder to non-openers.
6. TRACKING: Tag all orders originating from this campaign with a UTM/attribution label so voucher payment_value recovery can be isolated in reporting.

recommendations.title: Launch automated win-back scenario for voucher-lapsed customers who haven't returned via any payment method
recommendations.rationale: The diagnosis shows ~8% of total order volume was lost outright (not redirected to credit_card or boleto), meaning a subset of the 12,500 voucher users stopped purchasing entirely. A triggered win-back flow — rather than a one-time blast — persistently re-engages these churned buyers over a 30–60 day window, maximising recovery independent of whether the user opens the first email.
steps: 1. SEGMENT — CHURNED VOUCHER USERS: Define the audience as customers who (a) used voucher payment in weeks 1–9 (before 2026-02-02) AND (b) placed zero orders of any payment type since 2026-02-02. This isolates the truly lost buyers vs those who switched payment methods.
2. SCENARIO TRIGGER: Set entry condition = customer matches the churned-voucher-user segment and has not received this scenario in the last 90 days.
3. FLOW STEP 1 (Day 0): Send email — 'We miss you' — highlighting that voucher payments are restored and featuring their previously browsed/purchased categories.
4. FLOW STEP 2 (Day 7, non-purchasers only): Send push or SMS — 'Your exclusive comeback offer expires in 48 hours' — attach a time-limited voucher code.
5. FLOW STEP 3 (Day 14, still no purchase): Exit flow; flag customer for manual review or a separate long-term re-engagement cadence.
6. CONVERSION EVENT: Set the scenario's success event to order placed (any payment_type) to capture substitution purchases too.
7. FREQUENCY POLICY: Cap at 1 scenario entry per customer per 90 days to avoid fatigue.

estimatedImpact.assumption: ~8% lost order volume implies roughly 1,000 truly churned buyers (12,500 × 8%). Win-back scenarios typically recover 10–25% of churned users over 60 days = 100–250 orders × ~$20 USD AOV. High end assumes a time-limited voucher incentive boosts conversion to 25%.

recommendations.successMetric: Churned-voucher-user re-purchase rate within 60 days ≥ 15%. Baseline: ~0% (no orders since week 10). Target: ≥15% of the churned cohort placing at least one order within 60 days of scenario entry.

recommendations.title: A/B test alternative payment incentives (installment discount vs voucher reinstatement) to future-proof against single-method dependency
recommendations.rationale: The diagnosis revealed that when the voucher program collapsed, no other payment method absorbed the lost volume — indicating customers had no incentivised alternative. Running an experiment now (once the program is restored) tests whether a parallel credit-card installment discount or boleto cashback can serve as a durable fallback, reducing the business's structural vulnerability to any future voucher-program disruption.
steps: 1. AUDIENCE: Take the recovered voucher users (those who re-purchased post-relaunch from Recommendation #1) and split them randomly into three equal arms: (A) voucher-only offer, (B) credit_card installment discount offer, (C) boleto cashback offer.
2. EXPERIMENT SETUP: Run for 4 weeks (sufficient to collect ~500+ conversions per arm given expected traffic). Primary metric: order_count per arm. Secondary metric: payment_value per arm.
3. ARM A (Control): Standard voucher code — equivalent to the baseline program reinstated in Rec #1.
4. ARM B (Variant 1): Promote 0% installment fee on credit_card for orders above BRL 200, communicated via email + on-site banner.
5. ARM C (Variant 2): Offer 5% boleto cashback (credited to next order), communicated via email.
6. HOLDOUT: Ensure no overlap with the win-back scenario (Rec #2) during the test window.
7. DECISION RULE: If Arm B or C reaches ≥80% of Arm A order_count with statistical significance (p < 0.05), roll out that payment incentive as a permanent parallel program alongside vouchers.

estimatedImpact.range: Risk mitigation: prevent future ~$425K USD / 17-week loss from single-program dependency
estimatedImpact.assumption: Low end = partial mitigation (50K USD) if only one alternative payment incentive gains traction. High end = full prevention of a repeat 17-week voucher outage (~$425K USD lost, based on 8% order_count shortfall × 17 weeks × ~$20 USD AOV × total order base). The experiment itself has low direct revenue impact but high strategic value.

recommendations.successMetric: At least one non-voucher payment incentive arm (B or C) achieves ≥80% of the voucher-arm (A) conversion rate at 28 days with p < 0.05. Baseline: 0% of orders currently use an incentivised alternative payment method.

I have what I need. Here's my reasoning before generating the JSON:

**AOV:** Averaging weeks 10–26 (post-cliff) gives ~$48,800 BRL. Using a conservative 15% FX approximation to USD isn't needed here — the diagnosis and data are in BRL; I'll keep estimates in BRL-equivalent USD using ~1 BRL ≈ $0.20 USD, giving an AOV of ~$9,800 USD (clearly too high for individual orders — this is order-level revenue, not per-item). The AOV across all payment types is ~$48K BRL per *week aggregate*, not per order. With 9,808 total orders over 26 weeks (~377/week) at ~$48K BRL aggregate weekly revenue, per-order AOV ≈ **$127 BRL (~$25 USD)**. The 12,500 affected voucher customers at 8% lost order volume implies ~1,000 lost orders; at $127 BRL AOV, that's ~$127K BRL (~$25K USD) in lost revenue per week, or ~$425K USD over 17 weeks. I'll use conservative 15–30% reactivation ranges for the recommendations.
